channel.title: Nature Communications
channel.description:
Nature Communications is an open access, multidisciplinary journal dedicated to publishing high-quality research in all areas of the biological, health, physical, chemical and Earth sciences. Papers published by the journal aim to represent important advances of significance to specialists within each field.

We are committed to providing an efficient service for both authors and readers. Our team of independent editors make rapid and fair publication decisions. Prompt dissemination of accepted papers to a wide readership and beyond is achieved through a programme of continuous online publication.
channel.dc:rights: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.
channel.prism:copyright: © 2026 Macmillan Publishers Limited, part of Springer Nature. All rights reserved.

item.title: Template-free synthesis of colloidal quantum dot assemblies with molecule-like architectures
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70555-4

The template-free synthesis of colloidal assemblies with molecule-like architectures from colloidal quantum dots holds promise for optical applications but remains challenging. Here we report a one-pot synthesis of geometries resembling sp-, sp²-, and sp³-hybridized orbitals.
item.dc:title: Template-free synthesis of colloidal quantum dot assemblies with molecule-like architectures
item.dc:creator: Jiada Fan
Zhuang Ying
Jicun Ma
Jialiang Xu
Hui Cai
Jing Liu
Xinyue Wu
Chenhao Yang
Haorong Jiao
Qiulian Mao
Mei Chen
Liantuan Xiao
Yonggang Peng
Guofeng Zhang
Jiabin Cui
Mingyuan Gao

item.title: A hydro-topological strategy enables self-regulating biofilms for sustainable wastewater treatment
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70682-y

The moving bed biofilm reactor (MBBR) is a cornerstone technology in modern wastewater treatment, yet its performance is often hindered by carrier clogging, which significantly reduces overall treatment efficiency. In this work, the authors present a V carrier hydro topological design strategy that enables biofilm self-regulation, allowing simultaneous control of biofilm thickness and continuous hydraulic shear induced self-cleaning.
item.dc:title: A hydro-topological strategy enables self-regulating biofilms for sustainable wastewater treatment
item.dc:creator: Yong Fang
Zhiqiang Zhang
Boru Xue
Ying Liu
Kuichuan Sheng

item.title: Ultrastrong and tough paper structure from densified hybrids of multiscale cellulose fibers
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70357-8

Many sustainable alternatives to petrochemical plastics have unfavorable mechanical properties. Here the authors design a bacterial cellulose microgel for enhanced mechanical properties for sustainable packaging.
item.dc:title: Ultrastrong and tough paper structure from densified hybrids of multiscale cellulose fibers
item.dc:creator: Liqiong Liao
Bingbing Li
Zhiping Shi
Kai Li
Yao Lu
Yuxin Liu
Qi Zhou

item.title: Data-driven intelligent carbonization unifies diverse biomass into high-performance hard carbon negative electrodes
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70411-5

Biomass-derived hard carbon negative electrodes can cut cost, but properties vary by feedstock. Here, authors use data-driven intelligent carbonization to unify performance across diverse biomass, yielding high-capacity, long-life negative electrodes with lower footprints.
item.dc:title: Data-driven intelligent carbonization unifies diverse biomass into high-performance hard carbon negative electrodes
item.dc:creator: Junfeng Cui
Yi Rao
Jianbao Gao
Hao Zhang
Cheng Lin
Jiale Zhao
Jiawen Zeng
Chun Fang
Zhiqiang Wang
Jinyu Wen
Bo Song
Yunhui Huang
Haiping Yang
Jia Xie
Yonggang Yao

item.title: Strong interplay between polar and structural topologies
item.content:encoded:
Nature Communications, Published online: 13 March 2026; doi:10.1038/s41467-026-70515-y

Ordered antihedgehog polar lattices are engineered in antiferroelectric PbZrO3 thin films via interfacial dislocation networks. The authors reveal that these ordered topological structures originate from mismatch.
item.dc:title: Strong interplay between polar and structural topologies
item.dc:creator: Ru-Jian Jiang
Mei-Xiong Zhu
Su-Zhen Liu
Yu-Ting Chen
Desheng Ma
Yan-Peng Feng

Meng-Jiao Han

Yun-Long Tang

Xiu-Liang Ma
